Environmental Geography

Human-environment interactions, ecosystems, and contemporary environmental challenges.

Author

Geography Team

Welcome to the Environmental Geography module of Geography OpenCourseWare. This course bridges physical and human geography by examining the complex interactions between human societies and the natural environment — from ecosystem dynamics to contemporary environmental crises.

UGC NET Syllabus

  • Components: Ecosystem (Geographic Classification) and Human Ecology.
  • Functions: Trophic Levels, Energy Flows, Cycles (geo-chemical, carbon, nitrogen and oxygen), Food Chain, Food Web and Ecological Pyramid.
  • Human Interaction and Impacts, Environmental Ethics and Deep Ecology.
  • Environmental Hazards and Disasters (Global Warming, Urban Heat Island, Atmospheric Pollution, Water Pollution, Land Degradation).
  • National Programmes and Policies: Legal Framework, Environmental Policy, International Treaties, International Programmes and Polices (Brundtland Commission, Kyoto Protocol, Agenda 21, Sustainable Development Goals, Paris Agreement).

Biogeography

The spatial distribution of plants and animals.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗

TipDistribution Patterns
  • Island Biogeography Theory (MacArthur & Wilson): Species richness as a function of island area and isolation.
  • Zoogeographic Realms: Palaearctic, Nearctic, Neotropical, Ethiopian, Oriental, and Australasian.
  • Floristic Kingdoms: Boreal, Palaeotropical, Neotropical, South African, Australian, and Antarctic.
  • Endemism & Hotspots: Biodiversity hotspots — Western Ghats, Eastern Himalayas, Sundaland.
  • Migration & Dispersal: Animal migration routes and barriers to species dispersal.

Classification and distribution of soils

Content for Classification and distribution of soils coming soon.

Course Content

  • Ecology & Ecosystems
  • Biogeography
  • Environmental Degradation
  • Natural Hazards & Disasters
  • Environmental Policies & Governance
  • Sustainable Development

Ecology & Ecosystems

The structure and functioning of ecological systems.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗

NoteEcosystem Fundamentals
  • Ecosystem Components: Biotic (producers, consumers, decomposers) and abiotic (climate, soil, water) factors.
  • Energy Flow: Food chains, food webs, trophic levels, and ecological pyramids.
  • Biogeochemical Cycles: Carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, and hydrological cycles.
  • Ecological Succession: Primary and secondary succession — from bare rock to climax community.
  • Biomes: Tropical rainforest, savanna, desert, temperate, taiga, tundra, and aquatic biomes.

Ecosystem their management and conservation

Content for Ecosystem their management and conservation coming soon.

Environmental Degradation

Human-induced deterioration of the natural environment.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗

ImportantDegradation Processes
  • Deforestation: Causes, rates, and consequences — Amazon, Congo Basin, Southeast Asia.
  • Soil Degradation: Erosion, salinization, waterlogging, and desertification.
  • Water Pollution: Point and non-point source pollution, eutrophication, and groundwater contamination.
  • Air Pollution: Sources, types of pollutants, smog, acid rain, and particulate matter.
  • Biodiversity Loss: Habitat destruction, overexploitation, invasive species, and the sixth mass extinction.

Environmental education and legislation.

Content for Environmental education and legislation. coming soon.

Environmental hazards and remedial measures

Content for Environmental hazards and remedial measures coming soon.

Environmental Policies & Governance

National and international responses to environmental challenges.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗

TipPolicy Frameworks
  • Stockholm Conference (1972): The beginning of global environmental consciousness.
  • Rio Earth Summit (1992): Agenda 21, CBD, UNFCCC, and the principle of common but differentiated responsibilities.
  • Kyoto Protocol & Paris Agreement: Climate change mitigation frameworks and nationally determined contributions.
  • Indian Environmental Policy: Wildlife Protection Act, Forest Conservation Act, EIA notifications, National Action Plan on Climate Change.
  • Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA): Process, stages, and significance in project planning.

Environmental policy

Content for Environmental policy coming soon.

Human ecological adaptations

Influence of man on ecology and environment

Major gene pool centres.

Content for Major gene pool centres. coming soon.

Natural Hazards & Disasters

Geographic perspectives on natural hazards and disaster management.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗

NoteHazard Assessment
  • Earthquakes: Seismic zones, plate boundaries, and earthquake-prone regions of India.
  • Volcanic Eruptions: Types of volcanoes, eruption styles, and volcanic hazard zones.
  • Floods: Fluvial, pluvial, and coastal flooding — causes, frequency, and mitigation.
  • Cyclones: Tropical cyclone formation, tracks, intensity scales, and warning systems.
  • Droughts: Meteorological, hydrological, and agricultural droughts — vulnerability mapping.
  • Landslides: Trigger factors, susceptibility mapping, and early warning.

Principle ecology

Content for Principle ecology coming soon.

Soil erosion, Degradation and conservation

Content for Soil erosion, Degradation and conservation coming soon.

Soil profile

Content for Soil profile coming soon.

Sustainable Development

Balancing development with environmental conservation.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗

ImportantSustainability Frameworks
  • Brundtland Definition: “Meeting the needs of the present without compromising future generations.”
  • SDGs (Sustainable Development Goals): The 17 UN goals — a geographic perspective.
  • Ecological Footprint: Measuring human demand on Earth’s ecosystems.
  • Circular Economy: Waste reduction, resource efficiency, and cradle-to-cradle design.
  • Green Technology: Renewable energy, green infrastructure, and sustainable urban design.

Books and Further Readings

Environmental Geography provides the essential bridge between physical processes and human impacts. Explore Climatology and Oceanography for deeper physical science perspectives.
